does: This paper introduces ASSERT, an automated red teaming framework that generates diverse adversarial and safety-critical prompts to evaluate the robustness of large language models. The system employs three core techniques—semantically aligned augmentation, target bootstrapping, and adversarial knowledge injection—to create test suites that probe model behavior across semantic equivalence classes, related scenarios, and adversarial settings. The authors partition generated prompts into four safety domains and conduct fine-grained analysis across models, uncovering substantial performance variability of up to 11% absolute accuracy differences between semantically similar prompts and error rates reaching 19%, demonstrating that state-of-the-art safeguards fail to ensure consistency across closely related safety scenarios.
connects: This work extends the emerging research area of automated red teaming and adversarial evaluation for large language models, building on prior efforts in manual red teaming approaches and adversarial prompt generation. It responds to the recognized limitation that static safety evaluations on fixed benchmark sets provide incomplete pictures of model robustness, drawing from broader adversarial machine learning literature that emphasizes the importance of comprehensive coverage across input distributions. The paper combines semantic augmentation techniques with adversarial perturbation methods, uniting classical adversarial robustness evaluation with newer concerns about LLM safety and instruction-following reliability. This connects to earlier work on prompt injection attacks and safety evaluation frameworks while adapting those insights specifically to the challenge of evaluating consistent behavior across related inputs.
enables: This work establishes automated red teaming as a scalable approach to continuous safety evaluation, enabling systematic identification of gaps in LLM safeguards without requiring expensive manual annotation. The three-component methodology provides a template for generating diverse adversarial test suites that could be applied to emerging models or specialized domains beyond the initial safety focus. By demonstrating substantial robustness failures in production models, ASSERT motivates development of more robust training procedures and evaluation standards that account for semantic similarity and adversarial variants rather than isolated examples. The framework supports creation of larger, more comprehensive safety benchmarks and points toward automated adversarial training pipelines that iteratively identify and patch model vulnerabilities, laying groundwork for more reliable AI system deployment in safety-critical applications.
